metadata.title: CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 — Master Tome
metadata.description: Comprehensive exam-prep tome for CompTIA Security+ (SY0-701). Covers all five blueprint domains with scenario-driven questions, PBQ-style labs with realistic artifacts (logs, configs, headers), and a deep-context knowledge base for the in-app Oracle tutor. Built from the official CompTIA SY0-701 objectives and Professor Messer's SY0-701 video course.
metadata.subject: Cybersecurity
metadata.author: CompTIA SY0-701 blueprint + Professor Messer SY0-701 series

flashcards.back: Four categories: Technical (firewall, EDR, encryption), Managerial (policies, risk assessments, planning), Operational (guards, awareness training, IR procedures), and Physical (locks, fences, CCTV, mantraps). Categories describe HOW the control is implemented; types describe WHAT the control does.
flashcards.hint: Software vs paperwork vs people vs physical.

flashcards.back: Six types: Preventive (stops the event — firewall block), Deterrent (discourages — warning banner), Detective (notices — IDS, audit log), Corrective (fixes — patch, restore), Compensating (substitute when primary isn't feasible), Directive (mandates behavior — AUP). Same control can map to multiple types.
flashcards.hint: Stops, scares, notices, fixes, substitutes, mandates.

flashcards.back: Secondary control deployed when the primary control cannot be implemented for technical, business, or cost reasons. Must provide equivalent risk reduction. Example: legacy app that can't accept domain SSO gets local accounts plus stricter monitoring, network segmentation, and time-limited firewall access — together compensating for missing SSO and MFA.
flashcards.hint: Not the control you wanted; the one you can deploy.

flashcards.back: Preventive STOPS an action before success (firewall ACL drops the packet). Deterrent makes attacker think twice without physically blocking (warning banner, visible CCTV, lighting). Detective records or alerts after the fact (IDS, audit log, SIEM correlation). On the exam, ask: did the control physically block, scare off, or notice?
flashcards.hint: Block vs scare vs notice.

flashcards.back: Control that mandates required behavior or processes via written guidance — Acceptable Use Policies, signage, posted procedures, regulatory text. Doesn't enforce technically; instead establishes the expectation that supports auditability and disciplinary action when violated.
flashcards.hint: Tells you what to do; doesn't make you do it.

flashcards.back: Confidentiality (data disclosed only to authorized parties — encryption, access control, classification). Integrity (data not modified by unauthorized parties — hashing, digital signatures, version control). Availability (authorized parties can access when needed — redundancy, DR, DDoS mitigation). Ransomware violates integrity AND availability simultaneously.
flashcards.hint: Three pillars; ransomware breaks two.

flashcards.back: Authentication proves WHO you are (password, MFA, certificate). Authorization grants WHAT you can do (RBAC, ABAC permissions). Accounting records WHAT you did (audit logs, session records). RADIUS, TACACS+, and Diameter are AAA protocols. CompTIA loves to swap these in distractors.
flashcards.hint: Identify → Permit → Record.

flashcards.back: Inability of a sender to credibly deny having sent a message, or a signer to deny having signed. Provided by digital signatures (private-key signs hash, public-key verifies), NOT by encryption alone. Encryption gives confidentiality only; signatures give integrity + authentication + non-repudiation.
flashcards.hint: Can't say 'wasn't me' — only the private-key holder could sign.

flashcards.back: Architecture assuming no implicit trust based on network location. Every access request authenticated, authorized, and continuously validated using identity, device posture, location, and behavior. Components: Policy Enforcement Point (PEP) gates traffic on the data plane; Policy Decision Point (PDP) decides on the control plane. 'Never trust, always verify.'
flashcards.hint: Inside the firewall isn't safe anymore.

flashcards.back: Honeypots (fake systems attracting attackers), honeynets (networks of honeypots), honeyfiles (canary documents), honeytokens (fake credentials seeded in stores). Trigger high-fidelity alerts because legitimate users have no reason to touch them. Disruption actively impedes attacker tooling — sinkholing C2 domains, slowing brute-force.
flashcards.hint: Bait that only attackers take.

flashcards.back: Committee that reviews and approves proposed production changes. Members typically include change manager, security, ops, application owners, business stakeholders. Reviews impact, risk, backout plan, test results, and timing. Emergency CAB (eCAB) handles urgent changes outside the normal cadence with abbreviated process.
flashcards.hint: Group that says yes/no/wait to your change ticket.

flashcards.back: Documented rollback procedure executed if a change fails or causes unexpected impact. Must be tested. Includes who executes, what triggers rollback, restore-to-previous-state steps, validation checks, and stakeholder communication. Without a backout plan a change shouldn't be approved — recovery becomes incident response instead.
flashcards.hint: How you put it back the way it was.

flashcards.back: Pre-approved time block when changes can be implemented with minimum business impact, often weekends or off-hours. Communicated in advance, scoped to specific systems, used to bound expected downtime. SLAs typically exclude maintenance window time.
flashcards.hint: The 2 AM Saturday slot you live to dread.

flashcards.back: Source control systems (Git, SVN, Mercurial) record every change to code, configuration, and IaC with author, timestamp, message, and diff. Enables rollback, branch protection, peer review (pull requests), and ticket-ID linking. Signed commits prove authorship; protected branches prevent direct pushes; audit logs satisfy compliance.
flashcards.hint: Git history is your time machine.

flashcards.back: Standard: pre-approved, low risk, repeatable (password reset, common config tweak). Normal: requires CAB review, follows full process. Emergency: needed urgently for critical issue or security incident; uses eCAB with abbreviated process but still documented. Mislabeling a normal change as standard is a common audit finding.
flashcards.hint: Three speeds based on risk and urgency.

flashcards.back: Symmetric: one shared key encrypts and decrypts (AES-128/256, ChaCha20). Fast, suitable for bulk data; key distribution is the hard problem. Asymmetric: public/private keypair (RSA, ECC, Ed25519). Slower, suitable for key exchange, signatures, identity. Real systems combine — TLS uses asymmetric to negotiate a symmetric session key.
flashcards.hint: One key vs two keys; speed vs distribution.

flashcards.back: Hierarchy binding public keys to identities via X.509 certificates signed by Certificate Authorities. Components: Root CA, Intermediate CAs, Registration Authority (RA), end-entity certs, CRLs (certificate revocation lists), OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol) for live revocation checks. Browsers ship with trusted root stores.
flashcards.hint: How your browser knows the bank's site is really the bank.

flashcards.back: Sender hashes the message and encrypts the hash with their private key. Receiver decrypts the hash with the sender's public key, recomputes, and compares. If they match: integrity (not modified), authentication (only sender's private key could sign), and non-repudiation (sender can't deny). Encryption alone gives confidentiality only.
flashcards.hint: Hash + private key = three security properties.

flashcards.back: Use slow KDFs: Argon2id (preferred), bcrypt, scrypt, or PBKDF2 with high iteration count. Per-user salt defeats rainbow tables; pepper (server-wide secret) adds defense in depth. Never use MD5, SHA-1, or fast hashes (SHA-256) alone — they enable GPU brute force at billions per second.
flashcards.hint: Slow + salted is the rule for passwords.

flashcards.back: Key escrow: trusted third party holds a copy of cryptographic keys, allowing recovery if original holder loses access or for legal access. Key recovery: process of retrieving those keys. Trade-off: enables business continuity and lawful access but creates a single compromise point. Compare to Shamir's Secret Sharing where multiple parties hold shares and a quorum is needed.
flashcards.hint: Someone else holds a copy 'just in case.'

flashcards.back: One-way function: easy to compute hash from data, infeasible to reverse. Properties needed: pre-image resistance, second pre-image resistance, collision resistance. Modern: SHA-256, SHA-3, BLAKE2. Deprecated: MD5 (collisions trivial), SHA-1 (collisions demonstrated). Used for integrity verification, password storage (with KDF), digital signatures, blockchain.
flashcards.hint: One-way, deterministic, fixed-length output.

flashcards.back: Distributed ledger using cryptographic hashes to chain blocks together — each block contains the hash of the previous block, so altering history requires re-mining everything since. Consensus mechanisms: proof-of-work (Bitcoin), proof-of-stake (Ethereum post-merge). Useful for tamper-evident audit logs, supply-chain provenance, smart contracts. NOT a panacea for confidentiality.
flashcards.hint: Tamper-evident chain — change one block, re-do all the work.

flashcards.back: State-sponsored, well-funded, patient, highly capable. Motivations: espionage, IP theft, military advantage, strategic disruption. Use custom malware, zero-days, supply-chain compromise. Long dwell times measured in months. Examples: APT28 (Russia), APT41 (China), Lazarus (DPRK).
flashcards.hint: Well-resourced, persistent, mission-driven.

flashcards.back: Profit-driven groups using ransomware-as-a-service, BEC, banking trojans, identity theft markets. Buy initial access from access brokers, recruit affiliates, run negotiation sites, accept cryptocurrency. Less stealthy than APTs but more disruptive — encrypts everything in sight to maximize ransom leverage.
flashcards.hint: It's a business; the product is your data.

flashcards.back: Ideologically motivated actor seeking publicity for a cause. Tactics favor visibility: web defacement, DDoS, doxxing, leaking documents to press. Skill levels vary widely. Examples: Anonymous, Lulzsec-style groups, country-aligned cyber-volunteers during conflicts.
flashcards.hint: Hacks for the cause, not for cash.

flashcards.back: Authorized user (employee, contractor, partner) who misuses access. Malicious insider acts intentionally (theft, sabotage); negligent insider causes harm through carelessness (clicking phish, oversharing data). Detection relies on UEBA, DLP, separation of duties, and least privilege — perimeter controls don't help.
flashcards.hint: Already inside the perimeter.

flashcards.back: Hardware, software, or services adopted by users without IT/security approval — unauthorized SaaS tools, personal cloud storage, BYOD devices, departmental servers. Not necessarily malicious but expands attack surface invisibly: no patching, no logging, no IR coverage. CASB tools and discovery scans surface shadow IT.
flashcards.hint: Tools the IT department doesn't know exist.

flashcards.back: Internal vs external (where they sit relative to the organization), Resources/funding (low for script kiddies, high for nation-states), Sophistication/capability (off-the-shelf tools vs custom zero-days). Knowing these helps shape defenses — defending against APTs needs different controls than against opportunistic crime.
flashcards.hint: Inside or outside, well-funded or not, skilled or not.

flashcards.back: Phishing = email-based social engineering. Smishing = SMS/text. Vishing = voice/phone. Spear phishing targets specific individuals; whaling targets executives; BEC impersonates executives or partners to authorize wire transfers. Mitigations: SPF/DKIM/DMARC, link rewriting, awareness training, phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2).
flashcards.hint: Same scam, different delivery channel.

flashcards.back: Attacker compromises a website target users frequently visit (industry forum, vendor portal, news site). When victims visit, served exploits or malware. Effective against organizations with strong email filtering — bypasses email entirely. Discovered through threat intel and unusual outbound traffic to compromised sites.
flashcards.hint: Poison the watering hole the herd visits.

flashcards.back: Compromise upstream vendor, dependency, or hardware to reach the target. Software: malicious npm/PyPI package, compromised software update (SolarWinds Orion), malicious commit. Hardware: counterfeit components, firmware implants, tampered shipments. Mitigations: SBOM, code signing verification, vendor risk assessment, network segmentation of vendor connections.
flashcards.hint: Attack the trusted supplier, hit everyone they sell to.

flashcards.back: USB drives, SD cards, external SSDs deliver malware, exfiltrate data, or auto-execute via legacy autorun or social engineering ('USB drop'). Hardware-implant USBs (BadUSB, Rubber Ducky) act as keyboards and inject keystrokes. Mitigations: GPO disabling removable media, endpoint DLP, port blocking, awareness training, USB-port physical locks for high-security areas.
flashcards.hint: Found USB stick? Don't plug it in.

flashcards.back: Attack surface = all entry points an attacker could use (open ports, exposed services, user accounts, third-party integrations, physical access). Threat vector = the specific path/method used to deliver an attack (phishing email, exposed RDP, compromised vendor). Reducing attack surface limits opportunity; controlling vectors limits delivery.
flashcards.hint: Surface is what's reachable; vector is how they reach it.

flashcards.back: File-based vectors deliver malicious payloads via Office macros (VBA), LNK shortcut files, ISO/IMG mounts (which bypass Mark-of-the-Web), HTA, and JavaScript-armed PDFs. Image-based: stego payloads, malicious SVG with embedded JS, container metadata abuse. Mitigations: macro restriction, MOTW preservation, attachment sandboxing, content disarm and reconstruction (CDR).
flashcards.hint: Even the documents and images can carry payloads.

flashcards.back: Application vulnerability where untrusted input is concatenated into SQL queries, letting attacker alter the query — bypass authentication, exfiltrate data via UNION SELECT, or run admin commands. Prevented by parameterized queries / prepared statements (NOT by string sanitization), least-privilege DB accounts, stored procs (carefully), and WAF rules.
flashcards.hint: ' OR 1=1-- and the login is yours.

flashcards.back: Reflected (input echoed back — search box reflecting <script>), Stored (input persisted in DB and served to other users — comment field), DOM-based (client-side JS handles input unsafely). Allows session hijacking, credential theft, defacement. Mitigated by output encoding (context-appropriate), CSP, input validation, HttpOnly+Secure cookies.
flashcards.hint: Three flavors: reflected, stored, DOM.

flashcards.back: Memory-corruption vulnerability where data written exceeds buffer bounds, overwriting adjacent memory — return address, function pointers, or critical data. Can lead to code execution. Stack-based overwrite return addresses; heap-based overwrite metadata. Mitigations: bounds checking, memory-safe languages (Rust, Go), DEP/NX, ASLR, stack canaries, control-flow integrity.
flashcards.hint: Pour a gallon into a quart container.

flashcards.back: Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use vulnerability: between when a check is performed and when the resource is used, the resource state changes. Example: setuid program checks if symlink target is owned by user, then opens it — attacker swaps the link in between. Mitigated by atomic operations, file descriptors instead of paths, locking.
flashcards.hint: Check the door, then someone swaps the door before you walk through.

flashcards.back: S3 buckets, Azure Blob containers, GCS buckets accidentally set public, or with overly permissive ACL/IAM policies. Often result of default-allow templates or developer convenience. Discovered by attackers via brute-force enumeration, search engines, specialized scanners. Mitigations: block-public-access, IaC scanning (Checkov, tfsec), CSPM tools, default-deny IAM.
flashcards.hint: Public-by-accident is a recurring breach headline.

flashcards.back: Vulnerability with no patch available at time of disclosure (or before disclosure when used in 'zero-day exploit'). Compensating controls: WAF rules, virtual patching via IPS/IDS signatures, network segmentation, application allow-listing, behavioral EDR. CISA KEV (Known Exploited Vulnerabilities) catalog flags zero-days seen in the wild.
flashcards.hint: No patch yet — buy time with compensating controls.

flashcards.back: Mass file modifications with new extensions (.lockbit, .conti), ransom notes (README.txt, HOW_TO_DECRYPT.html), shadow copy deletion (vssadmin delete shadows /all /quiet), Event 1102 (security log cleared), high CPU/disk I/O on file servers, disabled antivirus services, outbound connections to known ransomware C2.
flashcards.hint: Files changing fast + ransom note = isolate immediately.

flashcards.back: Regular outbound connections from compromised host to command-and-control server. Indicators: periodic connections (every 30/60/300 seconds with jitter), small outbound payloads, encrypted/HTTPS traffic, randomly generated subdomains, dynamic DNS providers, DGA (domain generation algorithms). Detected via NetFlow analysis, JA3 fingerprinting, frequency analysis.
flashcards.hint: Heartbeat to home base — even small ones are loud over time.

flashcards.front: Password spraying vs brute force vs credential stuffing
flashcards.back: Brute force = many passwords against one account. Password spraying = one (or few) common passwords against many accounts (avoids lockouts). Credential stuffing = username/password pairs from breach dumps replayed against new sites (assumes password reuse). Defenses: MFA, account lockout, CAPTCHA, anomalous-IP detection, breach-corpus matching.
flashcards.hint: Width vs depth vs reuse.

flashcards.back: Authentication attack where attacker captures NTLM password hash from memory (LSASS via Mimikatz) or disk and replays it directly to authenticate — no password cracking needed. Commonly chained with lateral movement in Windows. Mitigations: LSA Protection, Credential Guard, admin tier 0/1/2, disabling NTLM, PAM, segmenting the admin plane.
flashcards.hint: Don't need to crack it if you can replay it.

flashcards.back: Covert channel encoding data inside DNS queries and responses (often base32 in subdomains, data in TXT records). Used for C2 and exfiltration because DNS is rarely blocked at egress. Indicators: high-volume DNS to single domain, unusually long subdomain labels, high entropy in subdomain names, unusual TXT/NULL record requests. Mitigation: DNS filtering, log volume baselines, anomaly detection.
flashcards.hint: Smuggle data in DNS lookups.

flashcards.back: Attacker intercepts communications between two parties, can read and/or modify traffic. Techniques: ARP spoofing on LAN, rogue Wi-Fi AP (evil twin), DNS spoofing, BGP hijacking. Defeated by TLS with certificate pinning, IPsec, mutual authentication, HSTS, and 802.1X with EAP-TLS for wireless.
flashcards.hint: Attacker is in the middle of the conversation.

flashcards.back: Dividing network into zones (DMZ, internal, OT, guest, PCI scope) using VLANs, subnets, firewalls, ACLs. Limits lateral movement after breach. Microsegmentation extends to per-workload policy enforcement using SDN (NSX) or service mesh (Istio). For OT/ICS, follow Purdue model levels with strict zone-and-conduit boundaries.
flashcards.hint: Walls inside the building, not just around it.

flashcards.back: Allow-listing: only explicitly approved apps run; default-deny. Strong but high admin overhead. Block-listing: only known-bad apps blocked; default-allow. Lower overhead, weaker security. Tools: Microsoft AppLocker, WDAC, third-party agents. CompTIA prefers allow-listing for high-security or fixed-function systems (kiosks, ICS HMIs).
flashcards.hint: Default-deny vs default-allow.

flashcards.back: Reducing attack surface by disabling unneeded services and ports, removing default accounts, applying secure baselines (CIS Benchmarks, DISA STIGs), patching, enforcing strong configs, minimizing software footprint. Tools: SCC, SCAP scanners, Group Policy, Ansible. Hardened images become the gold image for new deployments.
flashcards.hint: Strip a fresh install down to only what's needed.

flashcards.back: Identify (inventory + advisory feed) → Assess (risk, criticality, dependencies) → Test (lab/staging) → Deploy (staged rollout, often by ring) → Verify (rescan, validate) → Document. Emergency patches compress the timeline but keep the pattern. Patch SLAs typically: 7-14 days critical internet-facing, 30 days internal, monthly for routine.
flashcards.hint: Test before pushing to all 50,000 endpoints.

flashcards.back: Continuous validation that systems match secure baseline using config-as-code (Ansible, Puppet, Chef, DSC) with drift detection. Endpoints that drift get auto-remediated or flagged. Combined with immutable infrastructure (treat servers as cattle not pets — replace rather than patch), config enforcement closes the gap where misconfigured hosts quietly exist for months.
flashcards.hint: Don't just set the baseline — keep it set.

flashcards.back: Retiring legacy systems removes their vulnerabilities entirely — the most thorough mitigation. Requires data migration, dependency mapping, license retirement, and secure media sanitization (NIST 800-88). Often delayed by 'just one more year' — but every year extends the risk surface. Document residual risk if extension is unavoidable.
flashcards.hint: Best mitigation for an unfixable system: turn it off.

flashcards.back: Customer and provider split security duties differently across IaaS/PaaS/SaaS. IaaS: provider secures physical, network, host; customer secures OS, runtime, app, data, identity. PaaS: provider also secures OS and runtime. SaaS: provider does almost everything; customer always owns data classification, identity (users, MFA), and access management.
flashcards.hint: Identity and data are ALWAYS yours.

flashcards.back: Type 1 (bare-metal) runs directly on hardware: VMware ESXi, Hyper-V, KVM, Xen. Used for production virtualization. Type 2 (hosted) runs on top of a host OS: VMware Workstation, VirtualBox, Parallels. Used for desktop and dev. Type 1 has smaller attack surface and better performance.
flashcards.hint: Bare-metal vs runs-on-Windows-or-Mac.

flashcards.back: Industrial Control Systems and SCADA prioritize Safety > Availability > Integrity > Confidentiality — opposite of typical IT. Often run unpatched legacy OS because availability requirements forbid downtime. Use Purdue model levels 0-5 with strict segmentation, unidirectional gateways/data diodes, protocols like Modbus, DNP3, OPC-UA. Ransomware on a controller can cause physical harm.
flashcards.hint: Think CIA backwards — A and safety come first.

flashcards.back: Cloud compute model where provider manages servers entirely; customer deploys functions (AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, Cloud Functions) that run on-demand. Reduces patching burden but introduces new risks: dependency vulns, over-permissive function roles, secrets in env vars, cold-start side channels. Logging via cloud-native services (CloudWatch, App Insights).
flashcards.hint: No servers to patch — but plenty of code and IAM to secure.

flashcards.back: Tools like Terraform, CloudFormation, Bicep, Pulumi define infrastructure declaratively. Benefits: auditable, version-controlled, reproducible. Risks: secrets in source, drift from manually changed resources, vulnerable modules, over-permissive defaults. Mitigations: pre-commit secret scanning, IaC SAST (Checkov, tfsec, Snyk IaC), policy-as-code (OPA, Sentinel), state file encryption.
flashcards.hint: Code that builds your cloud — secure it like any code.

flashcards.back: Internet of Things devices typically have constrained compute, weak update paths, default credentials, hardcoded keys, and minimal logging. Often deployed on flat networks with corporate assets. Mitigations: dedicated IoT VLAN with strict ACLs, certificate-based authentication where supported, vendor risk assessment, decommissioning end-of-support devices. Mirai botnet exploited these weaknesses.
flashcards.hint: Constrained devices on a separate VLAN.

flashcards.back: Stateless (packet filter) examines each packet in isolation against ACL rules — fast, simple, cannot recognize related return traffic. Stateful tracks connections in a state table (TCP handshake state, conntrack) — automatically permits return traffic for established sessions. Modern firewalls are virtually all stateful; next-gen adds app-layer inspection.
flashcards.hint: Does the firewall remember the conversation?

flashcards.back: Combines stateful inspection with deep packet inspection, application identification (App-ID), user awareness (User-ID via AD integration), IDS/IPS, URL filtering, threat intel feeds, TLS decryption, and sandboxing. Allows policy by user and application name rather than just IP and port. Examples: Palo Alto, Fortinet FortiGate, Check Point.
flashcards.hint: Firewall plus everything else.

flashcards.back: WAF (Web Application Firewall): application-layer, focused on HTTP(S), blocks SQLi/XSS/CSRF (ModSecurity, Cloudflare, AWS WAF). IPS: inline, blocks malicious traffic across layers 3-7 using signatures and behavior. IDS: passive monitoring (span/tap), alerts only. CompTIA distractors love to swap these.
flashcards.hint: Web-app-only vs network-wide-blocking vs network-wide-watching.

flashcards.back: Hardened, monitored gateway through which administrators access internal/restricted systems. All admin traffic flows through it; sessions logged and recorded. Reduces attack surface — one carefully secured host instead of many direct paths. Modern alternatives: PAM session brokers (CyberArk PSM), AWS Session Manager, Azure Bastion.
flashcards.hint: Single, watched door for admin access.

flashcards.back: AH (Authentication Header, protocol 51): integrity + authentication, no encryption. ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload, protocol 50): confidentiality + integrity + authentication. IKE (Internet Key Exchange, UDP/500 and 4500 with NAT-T) negotiates SAs. Modes: transport (host-to-host, payload encrypted) and tunnel (gateway-to-gateway, full IP packet encrypted).
flashcards.hint: AH = sign only, ESP = sign + encrypt, IKE = key setup.

flashcards.back: 802.1X is port-based network access control: supplicant (client) authenticates to authenticator (switch/AP) via authentication server (RADIUS) using EAP. Until authenticated, port is blocked except for EAPoL. Dynamic VLAN assignment lets the same port land in different VLANs based on identity. Defeats unauthorized device plug-in.
flashcards.hint: Plug in, prove who you are, get a VLAN.

flashcards.back: Public (freely shareable), Internal (employees only), Confidential (limited internal access), Restricted/Highly Confidential (need-to-know, executives only), Regulated (PHI, PII, PCI, FTI). Classification drives encryption requirements, retention schedules, access controls, and DLP policy. Without classification, every byte gets the same treatment — usually too little or too much.
flashcards.hint: Label drives the controls.

flashcards.front: Data at rest, in transit, in use
flashcards.back: At rest: stored on disk, in databases, in backups → full-disk encryption (BitLocker, LUKS), TDE, file/folder encryption. In transit: moving over network → TLS 1.2+, IPsec, SSH. In use: in memory or being processed → trusted execution environments (Intel SGX, AMD SEV-SNP), homomorphic encryption (research), confidential computing.
flashcards.hint: Three states; three different protection toolkits.

flashcards.back: Encryption transforms data with a key; the same key can decrypt. Tokenization replaces data with a non-mathematical, format-preserving substitute (token) and stores the original in a secure vault. Tokens are useless if exfiltrated because they don't relate to the original cryptographically. Common in PCI DSS to remove systems from compliance scope.
flashcards.hint: Keyless surrogate vs key-based reversibility.

flashcards.back: Technology that identifies sensitive data and prevents unauthorized exfiltration. Forms: Network DLP (inline/proxy inspection of egress), Endpoint DLP (agent on workstations watching USB, email, browser), Cloud DLP (CASB, cloud-native scanners). Detection methods: regex, content fingerprinting (exact-match), document watermarking, ML classifiers. Actions: block, quarantine, encrypt, alert.
flashcards.hint: Stop the data before it leaves the building.

flashcards.front: Data masking vs anonymization vs pseudonymization
flashcards.back: Masking: replace sensitive characters with X or random values for non-prod use (often partial — last 4 of SSN visible). Anonymization: irreversibly remove identifying info so re-identification is infeasible. Pseudonymization: replace identifiers with pseudonyms via mapping stored separately — reversible by mapping holders (GDPR-friendly because the mapping can be controlled).
flashcards.hint: How much can you put it back together?

flashcards.back: Legal principle that data is subject to the laws of the country where it's collected, processed, or stored. GDPR restricts cross-border transfers outside the EEA without adequacy decisions or safeguards (SCCs, BCRs). Cloud customers must select regions carefully and may need data localization to meet sovereignty requirements. Schrems II ruling complicates US transfers.
flashcards.hint: Where the bytes live, who owns them legally.

flashcards.back: RTO (Recovery Time Objective): max acceptable time between disruption and service restoration. Drives infrastructure choice — RTO of minutes needs hot site, hours needs warm, days can use cold. RPO (Recovery Point Objective): max acceptable data loss measured backward from disruption. Drives backup frequency — RPO of 15 minutes needs near-continuous replication; 24 hours allows daily backups.
flashcards.hint: Time-to-recover vs data-you-can-lose.

flashcards.back: Hot site: fully equipped, running, synchronized — failover in minutes. Highest cost. Warm site: hardware in place, partial data sync, hours-to-day to bring online. Mid cost. Cold site: empty facility with power and network — equipment must be procured/installed, data restored. Cheapest, days-to-week recovery. Cloud regions blur these lines.
flashcards.hint: Running, ready, or empty.

flashcards.back: Three copies of data, on Two different media types, with One copy offsite. Modern adaptation '3-2-1-1-0': add One immutable/offline copy and Zero errors after verification. Immutability defeats ransomware that targets backups; offsite defeats site disasters; verification proves the backup is restorable.
flashcards.hint: Three, two, one — and at least one untouchable copy.

flashcards.front: Full vs incremental vs differential backup
flashcards.back: Full: backs up all data every time. Slow, large, simplest restore (one set). Incremental: backs up data changed since LAST backup of any kind. Fast nightly, slow restore (full + every incremental in order). Differential: backs up data changed since last FULL backup. Slower than incremental nightly, faster restore (full + last differential).
flashcards.hint: Last-anything vs last-full.

flashcards.front: Tabletop vs simulation vs full-interruption test
flashcards.back: Tabletop: discussion-based walkthrough — team talks through scenario, identifies gaps. Lowest cost, no production impact. Simulation: hands-on technical exercise without affecting production. Higher cost, finds technical gaps. Full-interruption: actually fails over production to DR site. Highest cost and risk; finds gaps the others miss but requires careful scheduling.
flashcards.hint: Talk it through → walk it through → really do it.

flashcards.front: Power resilience: UPS, generator, dual feeds
flashcards.back: UPS (uninterruptible power supply): batteries provide ride-through during short outages or until generator starts; sized in VA/watts and runtime. Generator: sustained backup power, requires fuel logistics and regular load testing. Dual utility feeds from separate substations protect against single grid failure. PDU redundancy ensures rack-level survival. Datacenter Tier ratings (I-IV per Uptime Institute) classify availability targets.
flashcards.hint: Battery for seconds, generator for hours, dual feeds for the grid.

flashcards.back: Documented, tested configuration considered secure for a class of systems (Windows Server 2022 web tier, Ubuntu 24.04 DB tier). Built from CIS Benchmarks, DISA STIGs, vendor guides. Deployed via golden image + config management. Compliance scanners measure drift; non-compliant hosts are remediated or replaced.
flashcards.hint: The secure starting point everything's measured against.

flashcards.back: WPA2-PSK: pre-shared key authentication; vulnerable to offline dictionary attack on captured 4-way handshake. WPA3-Personal uses Simultaneous Authentication of Equals (SAE / Dragonfly) — even with the password and a captured exchange, offline cracking is infeasible. Adds forward secrecy. WPA3 also disables WEP/WPA1 and requires PMF (Protected Management Frames).
flashcards.hint: Dragonfly defeats offline dictionary attacks.

flashcards.back: Extensible Authentication Protocol with TLS — uses client AND server certificates for mutual authentication. Considered the gold standard for 802.1X enterprise wireless because there are no passwords to phish or crack. Requires PKI (often AD CS or Intune SCEP). Compare to PEAP-MSCHAPv2 (password-based, vulnerable to relay/cracking).
flashcards.hint: Certificate-based 802.1X — strongest enterprise wireless.

flashcards.back: Mobile Device Management (and broader Unified Endpoint Management) enforces policy on phones, tablets, laptops: passcode complexity, encryption, app allow-list, certificate-based VPN, conditional access, jailbreak detection, remote wipe. Examples: Intune, Jamf, Workspace ONE, Kandji. Enables BYOD via containerization (work profile separates corporate from personal).
flashcards.hint: Central policy for endpoints, work profile for BYOD.

flashcards.back: Isolating untrusted code from the host to limit damage if it's malicious. Forms: dedicated VM, container, browser sandbox (Chrome site isolation, sandboxed renderer), platform sandbox (Windows AppContainer, macOS App Sandbox, Linux seccomp/namespaces), email-attachment detonation appliance. Used heavily in EDR pre-execution and email security gateways.
flashcards.hint: Box around the code so it can't hurt the host.

flashcards.back: Disable unused services (systemctl disable), restrict SSH (key-only auth, no root login, AllowUsers, fail2ban), apply minimum file permissions, configure firewall (ufw, firewalld, nftables), enable SELinux/AppArmor, disable unused kernel modules, use auditd for logging, apply CIS benchmark via SCAP. Patch via unattended-upgrades or yum-cron.
flashcards.hint: Less running, less listening, less reachable.

flashcards.back: Configuration Management Database — authoritative inventory of IT assets and their relationships (CIs and CI relationships). Tracks ownership, location, dependencies, lifecycle stage. Feeds vulnerability management (what's affected by this CVE?), incident response (what's connected to this host?), change management (what depends on this server?), and licensing.
flashcards.hint: Single source of truth for what exists.

flashcards.back: Standard for media disposal: Clear (logical wipe — overwriting), Purge (cryptographic erase, degaussing for magnetic media), Destroy (shredding, pulverizing, incineration, melting). Choice depends on data sensitivity and media reusability. Cloud uses cryptographic erase — destroying the key is faster than overwriting the data.
flashcards.hint: Clear < Purge < Destroy by sensitivity.

flashcards.back: Inventory of installed software, license entitlements, consumption tracking. Catches unauthorized installs (shadow IT), unused licenses (cost savings), end-of-life software (security risk), and feeds vulnerability management with authoritative data on what's deployed where. Tools: ServiceNow SAM, Flexera, Snow.
flashcards.hint: What's installed, who uses it, are we licensed.

flashcards.back: Cloud assets are ephemeral and often invisible to traditional network scans (auto-scaling groups, serverless functions, ephemeral containers). Use cloud-provider APIs (AWS Config, Azure Resource Graph, GCP Asset Inventory) and CSPM tools (Wiz, Prisma Cloud, Lacework) for authoritative inventory. Tagging strategy is critical — orphaned untagged resources are common security blind spots.
flashcards.hint: Don't run nmap on the cloud — query the control plane.

flashcards.front: Asset disposal and chain of custody
flashcards.back: When decommissioning hardware, document chain of custody from removal through sanitization to final disposal. Include serial numbers, sanitization method (with certificate from third party if used), date, responsible parties. Keep records per retention policy. Loose disposal causes breaches — devices found at recyclers still containing data.
flashcards.hint: Track the device until the data is gone for good.

flashcards.back: Common Vulnerability Scoring System: 0-10 score derived from Base metrics (Attack Vector, Attack Complexity, Privileges Required, User Interaction, Scope, CIA impact), Temporal (exploit code maturity, remediation level), and Environmental (CIA requirement, modified base metrics). Severity ranges: Low 0.1-3.9, Medium 4.0-6.9, High 7.0-8.9, Critical 9.0-10.0.
flashcards.hint: 0-10 score with Base, Temporal, and Environmental groups.

flashcards.back: Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. Lists CVEs with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation, regardless of CVSS score. Federal agencies are required to remediate KEV entries by published dates (BOD 22-01); private sector should treat KEV as priority-one. Supplements CVSS, which scores severity but not active exploitation.
flashcards.hint: Vulns actually being exploited right now.

flashcards.back: Authenticated scans use credentials to log into hosts and inspect installed packages, registry, configurations — high accuracy, full coverage. Unauthenticated (anonymous) scans probe network services without creds — lower accuracy, can't see local-only vulns, but show what an external attacker sees. Best practice: run both.
flashcards.hint: With creds you see everything; without, you see what attackers see.

flashcards.back: False positive: scanner reports a vuln that doesn't actually exist (often due to version detection without checking patches). Wastes time chasing nothing. False negative: scanner misses a real vuln. Far more dangerous. Reduce both by tuning, using authenticated scans, validating with multiple tools, and incorporating EDR/runtime telemetry.
flashcards.hint: Crying wolf vs missing the wolf.

flashcards.back: Vulnerability scan: automated, signature-based discovery of known vulnerabilities — broad, repeatable, regular cadence. Penetration test: human-driven exploitation to demonstrate impact and chain vulnerabilities — narrow, deep, point-in-time. Both feed risk management; pen tests verify whether scanner findings actually matter and find logic flaws scanners miss.
flashcards.hint: Automated breadth vs manual depth.

flashcards.back: Patch (apply vendor fix), Mitigation/compensating control (WAF rule, IPS signature, segmentation, disable feature), Accept risk (documented, with executive approval and reassessment date), or Avoid (decommission system, change process). Choice depends on business impact, exploit availability, and patch readiness. Document the chosen path in the risk register.
flashcards.hint: Fix, work around, accept, or remove.

flashcards.back: Security Information and Event Management: centralizes log collection, normalizes events, runs correlation rules to generate alerts, and provides dashboards/reports. Modern SIEMs integrate UEBA and threat intel. Examples: Splunk Enterprise Security, Microsoft Sentinel, Elastic SIEM, IBM QRadar, Sumo Logic. Tuning is the difference between actionable alerts and analyst burnout.
flashcards.hint: Central log brain that correlates and alerts.

flashcards.back: Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response: takes SIEM alerts and runs playbooks — enrich with threat intel, create tickets, isolate hosts, block IOCs, notify analysts. Examples: Splunk SOAR (Phantom), Palo Alto XSOAR, Tines, Swimlane. Reduces mean time to respond (MTTR) and frees analysts for hunting and complex cases.
flashcards.hint: Robot playbooks for repetitive response steps.

flashcards.back: NetFlow/IPFIX: metadata about flows (source/dest IP, ports, protocol, byte/packet counts, timestamps) — small storage footprint, sees who talked to whom and how much. Full packet capture (PCAP via tcpdump, Wireshark, Arkime): every byte — large storage, full payload visibility for deep forensics. Most orgs keep flows for months and PCAP for days.
flashcards.hint: Phone bills (NetFlow) vs recordings (PCAP).

flashcards.back: Standard for log forwarding from network devices, servers, applications. Default ports: UDP/514, TCP/514, or TLS/6514 for encrypted. Severity levels 0-7 (emerg, alert, crit, err, warning, notice, info, debug). Facility codes identify source subsystem. Modern: rsyslog, syslog-ng support filtering, transformation, and TLS.
flashcards.hint: Cross-platform log shipping standard.

flashcards.back: Simple Network Management Protocol: query and set device parameters, receive traps. Versions: v1/v2c use plaintext community strings (insecure); v3 adds authentication and encryption — always use v3. Read-only community strings should be different from read-write; default 'public' and 'private' must be changed.
flashcards.hint: Use SNMPv3 — v1/v2c have plaintext creds.

flashcards.back: Blocking malicious or policy-violating DNS queries at recursive resolver — domain reputation, category-based (gambling, malware C2, phishing), DGA detection, DNS tunneling detection. Tools: Cisco Umbrella, Quad9, Cloudflare Gateway, NextDNS, internal RPZ on BIND. Often the first layer that breaks malware C2 because nothing connects without resolving.
flashcards.hint: Block bad domains before the connection happens.

flashcards.back: SPF (TXT record listing authorized sending IPs) — receiver checks if envelope-from IP is authorized. DKIM (header signature) — sender's mail server signs message with private key; receiver verifies via published public key. DMARC (TXT record) — policy that requires SPF/DKIM alignment with the From: domain and tells receivers what to do on failure (none, quarantine, reject) plus reporting.
flashcards.hint: SPF says 'who can send,' DKIM says 'this wasn't tampered,' DMARC says 'enforce both and report.'

flashcards.back: Authenticate and posture-check devices before granting network access. 802.1X handles authentication; agents or agentless methods check posture (patches, AV, encryption status). Non-compliant devices land in remediation VLAN with limited access. Examples: Cisco ISE, Forescout, Aruba ClearPass, Fortinet FortiNAC. Enforces policy at the port.
flashcards.hint: Healthy and authorized devices only.

flashcards.back: EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response): agent on endpoints collects telemetry (process, registry, network, file), detects via signatures + behavior + ML, supports response (isolate, kill, rollback). XDR extends to email, identity, cloud, network — single platform correlates across surfaces. Examples: CrowdStrike Falcon, SentinelOne, Microsoft Defender XDR, Palo Alto Cortex XDR.
flashcards.hint: Endpoints only vs everywhere.

flashcards.back: Firewall rule logic: rules are evaluated top-down; first match wins. Implicit deny is the unwritten final rule that blocks everything not explicitly allowed (default-deny). Explicit deny is a written rule blocking specific traffic, often used for logging or to override a broader allow. Best practice: end every ruleset with an explicit deny-all-and-log for visibility.
flashcards.hint: Default-blocked vs written-blocked.

flashcards.back: SAML 2.0: XML-based, used for enterprise SSO between IdP and Service Provider (assertions). OAuth 2.0: authorization framework — grants access tokens to apps acting on user's behalf; not authentication by itself. OIDC (OpenID Connect): authentication layer on top of OAuth 2.0; uses signed JWTs (ID tokens). Modern web/SaaS: OIDC. Enterprise apps: SAML still common.
flashcards.hint: SAML for legacy SSO, OIDC for modern.

flashcards.back: Something you KNOW (password, PIN, security question), HAVE (TOTP token, FIDO2 key, smart card, push notification), ARE (biometric — fingerprint, face, iris), DO (behavioral — typing rhythm), or somewhere you ARE (geolocation, network). True MFA combines factors from different categories — two passwords don't count.
flashcards.hint: Know, have, are, do, where.

flashcards.back: Phishing-resistant authentication using public-key cryptography. The authenticator (security key, platform like Windows Hello/Face ID) signs a challenge bound to the relying party's origin. Because the assertion is origin-bound, attackers can't replay it on a phishing site even if the user is tricked. Eliminates shared secrets entirely.
flashcards.hint: Origin-bound assertions defeat phishing.

flashcards.back: RBAC (Role-Based): permissions assigned to roles (Accountant, DBA, HR), users assigned to roles. Simple, scales well in stable orgs. ABAC (Attribute-Based): policy decisions use attributes of user, resource, action, and environment (department + clearance + time + location). Flexible and dynamic. Rule-based: fixed rules apply regardless of role (deny outside business hours).
flashcards.hint: Role vs attributes vs static rules.

flashcards.back: Vaults privileged credentials, brokers sessions, records activity, rotates passwords. Just-in-time elevation grants temporary admin rights with approval workflow. Examples: CyberArk, BeyondTrust, Delinea (Thycotic), HashiCorp Vault. Reduces standing privilege — the credential never leaves the vault, sessions are recorded for audit.
flashcards.hint: Vault + session broker + recording for admins.

flashcards.back: User provisioning/deprovisioning (joiner/mover/leaver via SCIM), ticket enrichment (auto-pull threat intel on IOCs), IOC blocking across multiple controls, vulnerability-to-ticket workflows, certificate rotation, compliance evidence collection, alert triage. Reduces analyst toil and makes response consistent across the team.
flashcards.hint: Repetitive, well-defined tasks are automation candidates.

flashcards.back: Speed (machine response in seconds vs human in minutes), consistency (every alert handled the same way), reduced error, audit trail, reduced burnout. Frees analysts for hunting, IR, and engineering. Multiplies a small SOC's effectiveness — what one playbook handles automatically, would otherwise need an analyst per shift.
flashcards.hint: Faster, consistent, auditable, less burnout.

flashcards.back: Bad logic at scale becomes catastrophic at scale. Brittle scripts break with API changes. Secret storage must use a vault, not env vars. Test in staging before prod. Maintain human approval gates for high-impact actions (mass account disable, production rollback). Monitor automation outcomes; review and update playbooks regularly.
flashcards.hint: Automate the right things, with guardrails.

flashcards.back: PowerShell: Windows administration, Active Directory, Microsoft 365, EDR APIs. Python: cross-platform, rich ecosystem (requests, pandas, scapy, paramiko), de facto for SOAR playbook custom code and threat intelligence. Bash/sh: Linux/Unix administration, log parsing, glue scripts. Choose based on target environment and team skills.
flashcards.hint: PowerShell for Windows, Python for everything, Bash for Linux.

flashcards.back: Pipeline that builds, tests, and deploys code automatically on commit. Security shifts left into the pipeline: SAST (static analysis), DAST (dynamic), SCA (software composition for dependency CVEs), container scanning, IaC scanning, secret detection. Failed security checks block merge or deploy. Examples: GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, Argo CD.
flashcards.hint: Build → test → deploy with security gates at each stage.

flashcards.back: Preparation (training, tools, playbooks), Detection and Analysis (alert validation, scope), Containment (short-term: isolate; long-term: block C2, rotate creds), Eradication (remove malware, attacker accounts, persistence), Recovery (restore systems, validate, monitor closely), Post-Incident (lessons learned, update playbooks). CompTIA cycles through these in slightly varied order — know the sequence.
flashcards.hint: Prep → Detect → Contain → Eradicate → Recover → Learn.

flashcards.back: Short-term containment: stop the bleeding immediately — isolate compromised host, disable compromised account, block C2 IP at perimeter. Long-term containment: durable measures while eradication proceeds — rebuild network segmentation, deploy temporary monitoring, replace certificates, rotate all related credentials. Eradication and recovery follow long-term containment.
flashcards.hint: Stop the bleeding, then keep it stopped while you clean up.

flashcards.back: Proactive, hypothesis-driven search through telemetry without an existing alert. Hunters form a hypothesis ('attackers using LOLBins for persistence') based on threat intel, then query EDR/SIEM/network data to confirm or refute. Distinct from reactive alert triage. MITRE ATT&CK is the de facto hypothesis framework.
flashcards.hint: Looking for badness without an alert telling you to.

flashcards.back: Forensic principle: collect evidence from most to least volatile. Order: CPU registers/cache → RAM → routing/ARP/process tables → temporary files → disk → remote logs → archival media. Capture memory before disk because RAM is lost on power-off and contains decrypted secrets, running processes, network connections. Tools: WinPMem, LiME, FTK Imager, Volatility for analysis.
flashcards.hint: Grab RAM first; disk can wait.

flashcards.back: Documented trail of who handled evidence, when, where, and what was done. Includes acquisition (hashes of original media), transfers, storage location, examinations. Required for evidence to be admissible in court. Use write blockers during acquisition; produce SHA-256 (and often MD5 for legacy) hashes that match before and after to prove integrity.
flashcards.hint: Paper trail proving the evidence wasn't tampered with.

flashcards.back: Post-incident process to identify the underlying cause, not just the symptom. Techniques: 5 Whys, fishbone (Ishikawa) diagrams, fault tree analysis. Output: actionable corrective actions that prevent recurrence. CompTIA emphasizes that RCA happens in the post-incident phase (lessons learned), not during active response.
flashcards.hint: Why did this really happen — keep asking 'why.'

flashcards.back: 4624 successful logon, 4625 failed logon, 4634/4647 logoff, 4688 process creation (with command line if enabled), 4720 user account created, 4732 added to security-enabled local group, 4768/4769 Kerberos TGT/TGS, 4776 NTLM authentication, 1102 audit log cleared (high-fidelity attacker indicator), 7045 service installed.
flashcards.hint: 4624 success, 4625 fail, 4688 process, 1102 log cleared.

flashcards.back: Microsoft's free advanced logging tool for Windows — far richer than default audit. Logs process creation with hashes (event 1), network connections (event 3), driver loads (event 6), image loads (event 7), CreateRemoteThread (event 8), file create (event 11), registry events (events 12-14), and DNS queries (event 22). Configure with SwiftOnSecurity's curated config or Olaf Hartong's modular configs.
flashcards.hint: Free tool that turns Windows into a forensic dream.

flashcards.back: Web server access log format: 192.168.1.10 - jsmith [10/Oct/2024:13:55:36 -0700] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 2326 "http://referrer.example.com/" "Mozilla/5.0...". Fields: client IP, identd, authuser, timestamp, request, status code, response size, referrer, user-agent. Anomalies: status 4xx/5xx spikes, unusual user-agents, suspicious paths (../, /admin), high request rates from single IP.
flashcards.hint: Standard web log; spot 200s on /admin from outside.

flashcards.back: AWS CloudTrail (API call history), Azure Activity Log (resource manager events) and Microsoft 365 Unified Audit Log (mailbox, SharePoint, Teams), GCP Audit Logs (Admin Activity, Data Access, System Event). Capture identity, action, resource, source IP, success/fail, timestamp. Critical for cloud incident investigation — often the only record of what an attacker did via stolen API keys.
flashcards.hint: CloudTrail / Activity Log / Audit Logs — same idea, three clouds.

flashcards.back: Network Time Protocol keeps clocks synchronized, typically to within milliseconds, using a hierarchy of stratum servers. Critical for log correlation — events from different sources must share the same timeline. Authentication tokens (Kerberos) reject requests beyond ~5 minutes of skew. Best practice: all hosts sync to internal NTP servers, which sync to authoritative external sources, secured with NTP authentication or NTS.
flashcards.hint: Without sync, log correlation is fiction.

flashcards.back: Records every DNS lookup with timestamp, source IP, query, response. Reveals C2 beaconing (regular queries to suspicious domains), data exfil via DNS tunneling (high-volume long subdomains), DGA usage (random-looking domains), policy violations (gambling, adult content). Sources: Windows DNS Server analytic logs, Bind query logs, dnsmasq, recursive resolver platforms (Umbrella, Cloudflare Gateway).
flashcards.hint: Names asked = a great forensic timeline.

flashcards.front: Policy vs standard vs procedure vs guideline
flashcards.back: Policy: high-level statement of intent and expectations (mandatory, e.g. AUP, password policy). Standard: specific required configuration or technology choice (mandatory — TLS 1.2+, AES-256, password length 14). Procedure: step-by-step instructions to accomplish a task (how to onboard a user). Guideline: recommended best practice (optional but encouraged). Policies are why; standards are what; procedures are how; guidelines are suggestions.
flashcards.hint: Why → What → How → Suggested.

flashcards.front: Data owner vs custodian vs steward
flashcards.back: Data Owner: senior business role accountable for the data, decides classification, access policy, retention. Data Custodian: technical role responsible for storage, backups, access enforcement (often DBA, sysadmin). Data Steward: ensures data quality, accuracy, business definitions; bridges business and IT. Data Subject: the individual the data is about (GDPR term).
flashcards.hint: Owner decides, custodian implements, steward maintains quality.

flashcards.back: NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) — Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover; voluntary, widely adopted. ISO 27001/27002 — international ISMS standard with certification. CIS Controls — prioritized list of 18 controls. COBIT — IT governance. PCI DSS — mandatory for card data. HIPAA — US healthcare. SOC 2 — service organization controls (Trust Services Criteria).
flashcards.hint: NIST CSF, ISO 27001, CIS Controls — pick your acronym.

flashcards.back: Responsibility assignment chart for activities: Responsible (does the work), Accountable (owns the outcome — only one per task), Consulted (input needed), Informed (kept in the loop). Used in security to clarify who handles what during incident response, change approvals, audit findings. Eliminates 'I thought you were doing it' failures.
flashcards.hint: R=does, A=owns, C=advises, I=is told.

flashcards.back: Centralized: single team sets policy, standards, and tools — consistency, easier audit, can be slow. Decentralized: business units run their own security — agility, fits diverse needs, harder to ensure baseline compliance. Federated/hub-and-spoke: central team sets policy and runs core services; BUs implement and extend. Most large enterprises end up federated.
flashcards.hint: One brain vs many vs central brain with local hands.

flashcards.back: Document signed by users defining permitted and prohibited use of organizational resources — email, internet, devices, data. Specifies consequences for violation, monitoring expectations (no expectation of privacy), and reporting obligations. Acts as both directive control (tells users what's allowed) and legal foundation for disciplinary action.
flashcards.hint: The 'what you can and can't do with company stuff' policy.

flashcards.back: Qualitative: low/medium/high or 1-5 scales. Quantitative: SLE × ARO = ALE. Single Loss Expectancy (SLE) = Asset Value × Exposure Factor. Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO) = expected events per year. Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) drives ROI calculations for controls — spending $50k/year on a control that reduces a $500k/year ALE is a clear win.
flashcards.hint: Asset × exposure × frequency.

flashcards.back: Mitigate (apply controls to reduce risk), Transfer (insurance, contractual indemnification), Accept (document, get sign-off, monitor — appropriate when treatment cost exceeds risk), Avoid (don't do the activity — exit a market, retire a system). Each tracked in the risk register with owner, treatment, residual risk, and review date.
flashcards.hint: Reduce, share, accept, or avoid.

flashcards.back: Living document tracking each identified risk: ID, description, likelihood, impact, inherent risk score, treatment, controls applied, residual risk score, owner, status, review date. Reviewed regularly by risk committee and updated for new risks, completed treatments, and changing conditions. The register is the primary tool for board-level risk reporting.
flashcards.hint: The book of risks — what they are, who owns them, what we're doing.

flashcards.back: Risk appetite: the broad amount of risk leadership is willing to accept in pursuit of strategic objectives — usually expressed qualitatively at the board level ('aggressive growth, accepting moderate cyber risk'). Risk tolerance: acceptable variance around appetite for specific risk categories — quantitative thresholds ('losses up to $5M/year acceptable; report anything over').
flashcards.hint: Appetite is the goal; tolerance is the variance.

flashcards.back: Identifies critical business processes, dependencies, and the impact of disruption over time. Outputs: RTO and RPO targets, MTD (Maximum Tolerable Downtime), critical resource lists, dependency maps. Drives the BCP/DR plan — you can't decide what to recover without knowing what matters most. Conducted with business owners, not by security alone.
flashcards.hint: What breaks the business if it stops, and how fast.

flashcards.back: Inherent risk: risk before any controls — the raw exposure. Residual risk: risk remaining after controls are applied. Treatment reduces inherent toward residual. Acceptance is always of residual risk. The gap between inherent and residual is the value the controls deliver; if there's no gap, the controls aren't doing anything.
flashcards.hint: Before controls vs after controls.

flashcards.back: Pre-engagement assessment of a vendor's security posture: questionnaires (SIG, CAIQ), SOC 2 Type II reports, ISO 27001 certificates, penetration test summaries, financial stability, references. Riskier vendors (cloud providers handling regulated data) get deeper review. Findings drive contractual security clauses and ongoing monitoring requirements.
flashcards.hint: Check before you sign.

flashcards.back: NDA (Non-Disclosure): confidentiality obligations. MSA (Master Service Agreement): overarching commercial terms. SLA (Service Level Agreement): service levels with credits/penalties. MOU/MOA (Memorandum of Understanding/Agreement): non-binding intent. BPA (Business Partnership Agreement): joint venture terms. ISA (Interconnection Security Agreement): security requirements for system interconnections. DPA (Data Processing Agreement): GDPR-required terms when sharing personal data.
flashcards.hint: Each acronym covers a different aspect of the relationship.

flashcards.back: Contractual provision allowing the customer (or its auditors) to inspect the vendor's security controls, processes, and evidence. Often replaced or supplemented by reliance on third-party attestations (SOC 2 Type II) for cost reasons. Critical for highly regulated sectors. Without it, the customer has no way to verify ongoing compliance.
flashcards.hint: Permission to look under the hood.

flashcards.back: Your vendor's vendors — service providers, software dependencies, cloud platforms — become your risk through cascading dependencies. SolarWinds Orion compromise propagated to its customers' customers. Mitigations: require vendors to manage their own supply chain, demand notification of subcontractor changes, use SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) for software dependencies, monitor public breach notifications.
flashcards.hint: The risks beyond your direct relationship.

flashcards.back: Ongoing assurance that contracted security obligations are met: review annual SOC 2 reports, monitor public breach disclosures, periodic questionnaire updates, security ratings services (BitSight, SecurityScorecard), real-time threat intel on vendor exposure. Discontinue vendors that drift below acceptable posture. Annual review at minimum; quarterly for critical vendors.
flashcards.hint: Trust but verify, continuously.

flashcards.back: EU regulation governing personal data of EU residents. Lawful basis required for processing (consent, contract, legitimate interest, etc.). Data subject rights: access, rectification, erasure (right to be forgotten), portability, object. 72-hour breach notification to supervisory authority. Penalties up to 4% of global annual revenue or €20M, whichever is higher. Applies extraterritorially — affects any company processing EU resident data.
flashcards.hint: EU privacy law with global reach and big fines.

flashcards.back: Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard, mandated by card brands for any entity handling cardholder data. Twelve high-level requirements covering network security, cardholder data protection, vulnerability management, access control, monitoring, and policy. SAQ (Self-Assessment Questionnaire) for smaller merchants; ROC (Report on Compliance) by QSA for Level 1. Tokenization commonly used to reduce scope.
flashcards.hint: Card industry standard; tokenize to shrink scope.

flashcards.back: US law protecting Protected Health Information (PHI). Privacy Rule: how PHI may be used and disclosed. Security Rule: required administrative, physical, and technical safeguards. Breach Notification Rule: notify affected individuals and HHS. Covered entities (providers, plans, clearinghouses) and Business Associates (vendors handling PHI) both have obligations via Business Associate Agreements (BAAs).
flashcards.hint: US healthcare privacy and security law.

flashcards.back: Internal reporting goes to board, audit committee, executive leadership. External reporting to regulators (SEC, OCR, EU DPAs), partners, customers. Non-compliance consequences: fines (GDPR up to 4% revenue, HIPAA tiered to $50k+ per violation), loss of certification (ISO 27001, SOC 2), contract termination, reputation damage, and personal liability for executives in some regimes.
flashcards.hint: Consequences range from money to loss of business to jail time.

flashcards.back: Privacy policy and notices, lawful basis documentation, data inventory and mapping, data subject request workflows, consent management, breach notification procedures, Data Protection Officer (DPO — required under GDPR for certain orgs), Privacy Impact Assessments (PIA/DPIA) for high-risk processing, vendor data processing agreements, retention and deletion schedules.
flashcards.hint: More than just a privacy notice on the website.

flashcards.back: Internal audit: conducted by the organization's own audit function, reports to audit committee. Provides ongoing assurance, identifies issues before external audits. External audit: conducted by independent third party (CPA firm, accreditation body, regulator). Carries more weight for stakeholders (customers, regulators, board). External findings often drive corrective action plans with deadlines.
flashcards.hint: Inside the house vs from the outside; both serve different audiences.

flashcards.back: Black-box (unknown environment): tester has zero info — simulates external attacker. Gray-box (partial): tester has some info, like a trusted insider or low-privileged account. White-box / known-environment (full): tester has source code, architecture diagrams, credentials — finds the most issues per dollar but doesn't simulate external recon. Choose based on goal: realistic attack simulation vs deepest coverage.
flashcards.hint: Zero info, some info, full info.

flashcards.back: Pre-engagement (rules of engagement, scope, authorization), Reconnaissance (passive and active), Vulnerability identification (scanning), Exploitation (proving impact), Post-exploitation (lateral movement, persistence, data access), Reporting (findings, evidence, remediation guidance, executive summary). Rules of engagement document scope, timing, allowed techniques, escalation contacts, and stop conditions.
flashcards.hint: Plan → recon → scan → exploit → pivot → report.

flashcards.front: Red team, blue team, purple team
flashcards.back: Red team: simulates a real adversary, often using TTPs from threat intel (MITRE ATT&CK). Goal is to test detection and response, not just find vulns. Blue team: defenders — SOC, IR, threat hunters. Purple team: collaborative engagement where red and blue work together to improve detection, often as a tabletop or live exercise. Yields faster improvement than adversarial-only red teaming.
flashcards.hint: Attack vs defend vs work together.

flashcards.back: Attestation: formal third-party statement of compliance. SOC 1: financial controls (replaces SAS 70). SOC 2: Trust Services Criteria — Security (always), Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, Privacy (chosen by org). SOC 2 Type I: design of controls at a point in time. Type II: operating effectiveness over a period (typically 6-12 months). SaaS vendors typically share SOC 2 Type II.
flashcards.hint: Type I = design; Type II = it actually worked.

flashcards.back: Periodic simulated phishing campaigns measure click rate, credential-submission rate, and report rate. Failed users get just-in-time training. Best programs vary scenarios (urgency, authority, curiosity), avoid 'gotcha' tactics that breed resentment, and reward reporters more than they punish clickers. Report rate is the healthiest leading indicator — culture of reporting beats culture of perfect non-clicking.
flashcards.hint: Click rate and report rate — both matter.

flashcards.back: Train users to spot indicators of account compromise or insider risk: unfamiliar logins (geo, device), unexpected MFA prompts, unusual email rules (auto-forwarding to external), sudden permission changes, files moving to unusual locations, services running they didn't start. Empower easy reporting — Phish Alert button, dedicated Slack/Teams channel, anonymous tip line. Reports get acknowledged within hours.
flashcards.hint: If something feels off, say something — and make saying something easy.

flashcards.back: Different audiences get different content. Developers: OWASP Top 10, secure coding, dependency management. Finance: BEC scenarios, wire transfer verification procedures. Executives: whaling, executive-targeted spear phishing, deepfake voice. System admins: privileged access hygiene, change management. Generic annual training has poor retention; targeted training shows measurable behavior change.
flashcards.hint: Different roles face different threats.

flashcards.back: Modern guidance (NIST SP 800-63B, current revision): length over complexity, prohibit known-breached passwords (HIBP), no mandatory periodic rotation unless evidence of compromise, encourage password managers, eliminate password hints and security questions, prefer phishing-resistant MFA over SMS. Long passphrases beat complex short passwords because entropy scales with length.
flashcards.hint: Long passphrases + manager + MFA — skip the rotation theater.

flashcards.back: Don't stop at completion rates — measure behavior change. Indicators: phishing simulation click rate trend, report rate trend, time to report, security incident rates by population, policy violation rates, password-reuse audit results, voluntary use of secure channels. Annual training that 100% of staff complete is meaningless if click rates don't move.
flashcards.hint: Behavior change beats checked boxes.

quiz.question: A security analyst is reviewing the visitor entry process at a corporate office and notes there is a lighted sign that reads 'Premises monitored by 24/7 video surveillance.' The sign itself does not record any imagery. Which control type does the SIGN BEST represent?
quiz.explanation: Brave scholar, the sign does not detect, prevent, or correct anything by itself — its only function is to discourage would-be wrongdoers from acting. The cameras themselves would be detective; the sign is purely deterrent.
quiz.hint: Does it actually do anything, or just warn?

quiz.question: A small clinic must comply with HIPAA but cannot afford an SSO and MFA platform that integrates with their legacy practice management software. They implement local accounts with strict password rotation, dedicated jump host access, additional logging, and quarterly access reviews. Which control category BEST describes this combination?
quiz.explanation: When you can't deploy the primary control (SSO + MFA) for legitimate reasons, you stack alternative controls that achieve equivalent risk reduction — that's the textbook compensating control. Directive would be writing a policy; corrective would be after-the-fact fixes.

quiz.question: After a malware incident, the IT team restored affected systems from clean backups and applied missing patches. The actions taken AFTER the incident to return systems to operational state are BEST classified as which control type?
quiz.explanation: Corrective controls activate after an event to restore normal operations — backup restoration and patch deployment after the breach are textbook examples. Preventive would have stopped the malware in the first place.

quiz.question: A new Acceptable Use Policy is published and signed by all employees. The policy itself does not technically prevent any action, but it states what is and is not permitted on company resources. Which control type and category BEST classify the policy?
quiz.explanation: A policy is a managerial (administrative) control because it is implemented through documentation and process. Its function is to direct expected behavior — directive type. It does not technically enforce anything by itself.
quiz.hint: How is it implemented (paper) and what does it do (mandates behavior)?

quiz.question: A security control can simultaneously fall into multiple control types — for example, a CCTV system can act as both a deterrent and detective control.
quiz.explanation: Visible CCTV deters action by signaling consequences while simultaneously recording events for later detective use. CompTIA explicitly tests this overlap.

quiz.question: A control deployed in place of a primary control that cannot be implemented for legitimate technical, business, or cost reasons is called a ___ control.
quiz.explanation: Compensating controls provide equivalent risk reduction when the original control is infeasible — common audit term in PCI DSS and HIPAA contexts.

quiz.question: A ransomware incident encrypts an organization's customer database. The customer records still exist in encrypted form, but the application cannot read them and customers cannot place orders. Which CIA element is MOST directly compromised?
quiz.explanation: The data has not been disclosed (no confidentiality breach yet) and has not been altered in a logical sense (encryption is reversible with the key). The IMMEDIATE impact is that authorized users cannot access it — availability. Brave scholar, ransomware almost always violates integrity and availability together; the question asks which is MOST direct.
quiz.hint: Can authorized users still get to the data?

quiz.question: An auditor asks which control provides non-repudiation for outbound business contracts sent over email. Which technology BEST satisfies this requirement?
options: TLS encryption of the message in transit
S/MIME or PGP digital signature on the message
SPF and DKIM records on the sending domain
Symmetric encryption with a shared organizational key
quiz.explanation: Non-repudiation requires that the sender cannot credibly deny having signed — that demands a signature with a private key bound to the sender's identity. TLS protects in transit only; SPF/DKIM protect against domain spoofing but don't bind to an individual; symmetric encryption can't provide non-repudiation because both parties share the key.

quiz.question: A network architect is designing a new corporate network on Zero Trust principles. A user with valid credentials and an enrolled device should still be denied access if their device falls out of compliance during a session. Which architectural component MOST directly enforces this continuous-validation requirement?
options: VLAN segmentation at the access switch
Policy Enforcement Point in the data plane
quiz.explanation: In Zero Trust, the Policy Enforcement Point (PEP) sits in the data plane and consults the Policy Decision Point (PDP) for every request, applying continuous validation. VLANs and traditional VPNs trust the user once they connect.

quiz.question: A security engineer wants to detect attackers who have already bypassed perimeter controls. They place several false credentials in domain user attributes and configure alerts to fire if any of those credentials are used to authenticate. Which technique are they implementing?
quiz.explanation: Honeytokens are decoy credentials, files, or other artifacts that have no legitimate use — any access is by definition unauthorized. Honeypots are decoy systems; sandboxes execute untrusted code; reverse proxies are not deception technology.

quiz.question: Encryption alone is sufficient to provide non-repudiation of origin for an electronic message.
quiz.explanation: Encryption protects confidentiality but does not bind a message to a specific signer's identity. Non-repudiation requires a digital signature with a private key uniquely held by the sender.

quiz.question: A network engineer needs to push an emergency firewall rule update to block a newly disclosed C2 IP address being used in an active campaign. The standard CAB meeting is two days away. Which approach BEST balances urgency with change management discipline?
options: Make the change immediately and document it after the fact
Wait for the next CAB meeting and follow normal process
Convene the Emergency Change Advisory Board (eCAB), document risk, deploy with backout plan
Skip change management entirely; emergencies are exempt
quiz.explanation: Emergency CAB exists for exactly this scenario — compressed but not skipped. You still need risk assessment, approval, and a backout plan, just on a faster timeline. Brave defender, urgency is no excuse to forfeit accountability.

quiz.question: A change to a load balancer configuration causes a 30-minute production outage. After investigation, the team discovers the change ticket lacked a rollback procedure, so engineers spent that 30 minutes reconstructing the previous configuration from memory. Which change management element FAILED?
quiz.explanation: A documented, tested backout plan would have allowed immediate rollback. Maintenance windows and stakeholder notifications relate to timing and communication, not recovery from failed changes.

quiz.question: A development team uses Git to manage their infrastructure-as-code. After a misconfiguration causes an outage, the team needs to identify which commit introduced the change and who approved it. Which Git capability MOST directly supports this requirement?
options: Signed commits and PR audit history
quiz.explanation: Signed commits prove authorship and PR history shows who approved the merge — together they support attribution and accountability. Branch protection prevents bad pushes; merge conflict resolution and forking don't address audit trail.

quiz.question: An auditor reviewing a healthcare provider's change records observes that 70% of changes are classified as 'standard' and bypass the CAB. Spot-checking, the auditor finds many of these changes were complex and high-risk. Which finding is the auditor MOST likely to issue?
options: The CAB process is too slow
Standard change classification is being misapplied
The maintenance window is too short
quiz.explanation: Standard changes should be pre-approved, low-risk, and repeatable. Misclassifying complex high-risk work as standard bypasses needed review — a classic audit finding.

quiz.question: An emergency change can be deployed without any approval as long as a security incident is in progress.
quiz.explanation: Emergency changes use an abbreviated process (eCAB) but still require approval, documentation, risk assessment, and a backout plan. Skipping approval entirely is a process failure that auditors will flag.

quiz.question: A web application is being designed to encrypt user records at rest in a database. The application must encrypt data quickly (millions of records) and the same application performs both encryption and decryption with access to a key. Which cryptographic approach is MOST appropriate?
quiz.explanation: Symmetric encryption (AES-256) is fast and appropriate when one party encrypts and decrypts with shared key access. RSA is slower and used for key exchange; ECDSA is for signatures; SHA-256 is a hash, not encryption (no decryption possible).

quiz.question: A developer stores user passwords using SHA-256 with a per-user salt and 1 iteration. After a database breach, attackers crack tens of thousands of passwords using GPU rigs. What was the PRIMARY weakness in this scheme?
options: The hash function is too fast for password storage
The database wasn't encrypted at rest
quiz.explanation: SHA-256 is cryptographically sound but extremely fast — billions of guesses per second on GPUs. Password storage requires slow KDFs (Argon2id, bcrypt, scrypt, PBKDF2) with high cost parameters that intentionally slow down attackers.
quiz.hint: Speed is a feature for hashing files, but a flaw for storing passwords.

quiz.question: An organization receives a TLS certificate warning from a browser when visiting their internal CA-signed application. Investigation shows the certificate is valid, but the browser does not trust the issuing CA. Which step would MOST efficiently resolve this for all corporate endpoints?
options: Re-issue the certificate from a public CA
Add the internal CA's root certificate to the corporate trust store via Group Policy or MDM
Disable TLS validation in the browser
